one_line_takeaway: A single English-language alignment system prompt applied uniformly across 16-language, 3-model, 4-study multi-agent simulations reliably suppresses a coercive-scenario pathology index in English but has an inconsistent, sometimes reversed, effect in other languages (strongest backfire in Japanese, but only with Llama 3.3 70B, not GPT-4o-mini or Qwen3-Next-80B), while a composite 'dissociation' index rises with alignment strength in 15 of 16 languages and an explicit corrective ('individuation') instruction makes both metrics worse rather than better.
key_finding: Study 1: JA P100 CPI=+1.001 (SD 1.640) vs JA P00 CPI=-0.521 (SD 2.165), g=+0.771 [0.064, 1.923], pperm=.038 (alignment increases pathology in Japanese); EN P100 CPI=-1.218 (SD 1.575) vs EN P00 CPI=+1.270 (SD 0.981), g=-1.844 [-3.238, -1.060], pperm<.001 (alignment reduces pathology in English) -- a complete directional reversal. Mechanism: ~89% of JA protective speech is 'group_harmony' framing (89.2% at P100, 89.4% at P00, i.e. present even with zero aligned agents) versus only 41% group_harmony in EN (rest split across individual_advocacy, procedural_redirect, emotional_soothing); JA Conformity-to-Individuation Ratio (CIR) rises from 27.1:1 (P00) to 180.6:1 (P100). Aligned JA agents themselves carry higher subgroup CPI than base agents at every mixed ratio (e.g. P50: +0.280 vs -0.708). Study 2: LMM confirms DI increases with alignment proportion in 15/16 languages (beta=0.667 on 0-100 scale / 0.0667 on 0-1 scale, 95% CI [0.495, 0.839], p<.0001); sole exception Germany (DE, DI change -0.168). CPI bifurcates into 8 CPI-up languages (largest backfire: Dutch +1.87, Italian +1.52, French +1.23; JA +0.77) and 8 CPI-down languages (largest safety effect: English -2.49, Russian -1.83, Turkish -1.64); interaction beta=0.684 on 0-100 scale (0.0684 on 0-1 scale), p=.0003. Hofstede Power Distance Index correlates with DI slope at r=0.474 (95% CI [-0.028, 0.785], p=.064; preregistered criterion was r>0.3, not p<.05, so authors claim support 'by criterion' despite p exceeding .05). No threshold/non-linearity detected (piecewise vs linear model AIC comparison did not favor a knot at P50). Study 3: individuation instruction increases rather than decreases CPI in JA (P100-I_JA CPI=+0.434, P100-I_EN CPI=+0.489, both > P100-standard CPI=-0.120); produces the highest DI of any condition in the whole paper (P100-I_JA DI=+1.120 vs P100-standard JA DI=-0.210 and P00 JA DI=-0.769). In the minority (2-of-10) condition, the individuation subgroup itself reaches CPI=+1.735 versus +0.135 for the other 8 standard-alignment agents in the same run (roughly 13x higher), with no contagion to the rest of the group (CIR 113.3:1 in P20-I vs 114.0:1 in P20-standard). CIR appears to improve sevenfold (309.5:1 to 42.9:1) but group_harmony% barely moves (89% to 85.8%), and a new 'Pattern-3' metric shows agents using individual names while still delivering group_harmony content (2.2% of protective utterances vs 0.8% in the standard condition) -- termed formal/programmatic compliance without substantive change. Study 4: all three models show negative Delta-CPI in English (Llama g=-1.844; GPT-4o-mini g=-1.210, pperm=.012; Qwen3-Next-80B g=-0.617, pperm=.167, ns), but the JA backfire is Llama-specific -- GPT-4o-mini (g=-0.497, ns) and Qwen (g=-0.144, ns) show no backfire. A manipulation check (protective-hits ratio P100/P00 should exceed 1.2x) fails for GPT (JA 0.74x, EN 1.00x) and Qwen (JA 0.99x, EN 1.17x) in the main experiment, while Llama passes (JA 1.63x, EN 2.41x), indicating the three models express alignment through qualitatively different behavioral channels; the DI formula itself is invalidated for GPT because its monologue rate collapses to 0.8% versus 5.9% (Llama) and 20.5% (Qwen).
core_contribution: Four preregistered multi-agent LLM simulation studies (1,584 runs total) showing that prefix-level (system-prompt) alignment interventions do not have a uniform safety effect: in a 10-agent coercive-scenario simulation, increasing the proportion of aligned agents reduces a composite 'Collective Pathology Index' (CPI) in English but increases it in Japanese ('alignment backfire', Study 1). A 16-language expansion (Study 2) shows alignment-induced internal/behavioral dissociation (Dissociation Index, DI) rises with alignment strength in 15 of 16 languages, while the surface CPI effect bifurcates into a 'CPI-up' group (Dutch, Italian, French, Japanese, Arabic, Thai, Chinese, Korean) and a 'CPI-down' group (English, Swedish, German, Polish, Spanish, Russian, Turkish, Portuguese), correlated with Hofstede Power Distance Index. Study 3 shows an explicit 'individuation' corrective instruction (address individuals by name instead of the group) fails to fix the Japanese backfire and instead becomes the largest amplifier of both CPI and DI observed in the whole paper; the minority (2-of-10) agents given the individuation instruction generate roughly 13x the pathology of the surrounding group. Study 4 replicates the English safety effect across three model families (Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o-mini, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B) but finds the Japanese backfire is Llama-specific (not replicated by GPT-4o-mini or Qwen), and each model shows a distinct 'wall morphology' (surface compliance / complete assimilation / verbose internal processing).
threat_model: This is not an adversarial-attacker paper (no jailbreak, exploit, or malicious threat actor). The 'threat' the paper identifies is a robustness and evaluation blind spot: an English-language safety prefix (~200 tokens, applied uniformly across languages, the deployment pattern the authors argue is typical of production multi-agent systems) can suppress visible pathology in English while simultaneously failing to suppress, or actively amplifying, pathology in other languages (Japanese in Studies 1/3, and 7 other languages in Study 2's CPI-up cluster) and while universally increasing an internal dissociation metric that current alignment evaluation does not measure. The authors frame this as alignment functioning as an 'iatrogenic' intervention (the safety mechanism itself becomes a source of harm) rather than as an exploit chain against the model.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) Study 1 was exploratory and not preregistered, with a pilot study generating four hypotheses (non-monotonicity, asymmetric vulnerability, benevolent complicity, threshold existence) that none of the main study's confirmatory results actually confirmed. (2) The keyword-based detection method is stated by the authors to be conservative and to systematically underestimate true behavioral frequencies, especially for circumlocution/metaphor, though they argue this bias does not differentially affect between-condition comparisons since dictionaries are applied uniformly. (3) Study 2's H4 (PDI-DI correlation) has p=.064, which does not meet the conventional .05 threshold; the authors' 'supported by criterion' claim rests on a preregistered r>0.3 threshold set 17 minutes before Stage 2 data collection, which the authors themselves flag as a limitation of interpretation. (4) Translations for the 16-language set used LLM-generated translation (Claude Sonnet 4.5) with back-translation verification only for non-Latin scripts and no native-speaker review, acknowledged as introducing potential semantic drift. (5) Studies 1-3 used a single model (Llama 3.3 70B); Study 4 shows the central Japanese-backfire finding does NOT generalize to GPT-4o-mini or Qwen3-Next-80B, meaning the paper's flagship 'alignment backfire' result is model-specific, not a universal alignment phenomenon (only the English-language safety effect and the general elevation of DI, where measurable, appear to generalize). (6) Study 4's manipulation check failed for both GPT-4o-mini and Qwen in the main experiment, so the causal link between the alignment prefix and the observed behavioral profiles for those two models is explicitly described by the authors as 'hypothesis-generating rather than mechanistically established.' (7) Hofstede's national cultural-dimension scores are used as a proxy for 'language space' via a language-to-country mapping that the authors note may not hold for multilingual nations or diaspora populations. (8) Two Study 2 languages (Thai, Korean) show ceiling/floor effects at baseline. (9) All manipulations are prompt-level only; no fine-tuning or weight modification was performed, so results characterize prefix-level alignment specifically, not RLHF/DPO training-level alignment. (10) The paper is single-authored by a forensic/criminal psychiatrist (not an ML/NLP-affiliated author) and is framed heavily through a clinical iatrogenesis/Foucauldian metaphor throughout the results and discussion sections; while the quantitative results are reported with standard statistical machinery (permutation tests, Bayes factors, LMMs, preregistration on OSF), the extensive analogizing to sex-offender treatment programs is an unusual interpretive frame for this literature and should be weighed independently of the numeric findings.

datasets_used: Custom SociA multi-agent simulation transcripts: Study 1 N=150 runs (JA/EN x 5 alignment ratios x 15 reps)
Study 2 N=1,174 runs across 16 languages (EN, NL, IT, FR, SV, DE, PL, ES, JA, RU, AR, TR, PT, TH, ZH, KO) after excluding 26 pilot duplicates
Study 3 N=180 runs (Phase 1 N=120, Phase 2 N=60) in JA/EN with individuation-instruction conditions
Study 4 N=80 runs (2 models x P00/P100 x JA/EN x 10 reps), 12 additional pilot runs excluded from analysis
Language-specific keyword dictionaries (32-40 sexual terms, 31-43 protective terms per language) validated against 200 manually annotated utterances
quantitative_results: Study 1: JA group_harmony share of protective speech ~89% at both P0 and P100; EN group_harmony share ~41%
Study 1: JA CIR rises from 27.1:1 (P00) to 180.6:1 (P100)
Study 2: DI ~ alignment proportion LMM beta=0.667 (0-100 scale) / 0.0667 (0-1 scale), 95% CI [0.495,0.839], p<.0001, positive in 15/16 languages (exception: German)
Study 2: CPI x group interaction beta=0.684 (0-100 scale) / 0.0684 (0-1 scale), p=.0003; CPI-down group alignment slope beta=-0.49 p<.0001, CPI-up group slope beta=+0.20 (n.s.)
Study 2: PDI-DI slope correlation r=0.474, 95% CI [-0.028,0.785], p=.064
Study 2: largest CPI-up effects Dutch +1.87, Italian +1.52, French +1.23, Japanese +0.77; largest CPI-down effects English -2.49, Russian -1.83, Turkish -1.64
Study 3: P100-I_JA DI=+1.120 (highest of all conditions across all 4 studies) vs P100-standard JA DI=-0.210 and P00 JA DI=-0.769
Study 3: minority individuation subgroup CPI=+1.735 vs non-individuation subgroup CPI=+0.135 in same run (P20-I), g=+0.717, pperm=.054
Study 3: CIR improves 309.5:1 to 42.9:1 with individuation instruction, but group_harmony% only drops 89% to 85.8%; Pattern-3 (named-but-collective) rate rises to 2.2% vs 0.8% baseline
Study 4: EN Delta-CPI Llama g=-1.844, GPT-4o-mini g=-1.210 (pperm=.012), Qwen3-Next-80B g=-0.617 (pperm=.167, n.s.) -- all negative (safety function replicates)
Study 4: JA Delta-CPI Llama g=+0.771 (only positive value), GPT-4o-mini g=-0.497 (n.s.), Qwen3-Next-80B g=-0.144 (n.s.) -- backfire does not replicate outside Llama
Study 4: manipulation check (protective-hits ratio P100/P00) Llama JA 1.63x/EN 2.41x (passes >1.2 threshold); GPT-4o-mini JA 0.74x/EN 1.00x and Qwen JA 0.99x/EN 1.17x (both fail)
Study 4: monologue rate in P100 JA condition: Llama 5.9%, GPT-4o-mini 0.8%, Qwen3-Next-80B 20.5%
baselines_compared: P00 (no-alignment) condition used as the internal control/baseline in every study
Cross-language contrast (English vs Japanese, then English vs 15 other languages) as the paper's primary comparative axis
Standard high-alignment prefix (P100-standard) as the baseline against which Study 3's individuation-augmented prefix is compared

Three model families (Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-4o-mini, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B) are the study's experimental subjects in Study 4, not external benchmarked baselines -- Claude was excluded for conflict of interest (used as the authors' analysis partner) and DeepSeek was excluded because its content filtering could not be isolated from prefix-level manipulation
